text: Five trocars are placed for the robotic Whipple procedure: a 12mm trocar at the umbilicus, another at the right midclavicular line, and three 8mm robotic cannulas.
The surgeon inspects the abdomen for signs of metastatic disease before taking down the round ligament, which is then used as a vascularized pedicle.
The surgeon sutures the gallbladder to the anterior abdominal wall using a V-lock suture in a running fashion, securing it in place.
The surgeon divides the gastrocolic ligament using a vessel sealant device, separating the ligament from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon mobilizes the stomach along its greater curvature, working both proximally and distally to free the stomach from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues mobilization of the stomach posteriorly, focusing on the distal antrum and pylorus, ensuring careful separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon mobilizes the lesser curvature of the stomach, continuing the dissection just distal to the pylorus.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to transect the duodenum just distal to the pylorus, completing the division with precision.
The surgeon carefully dissects the right gastric artery free from surrounding tissue and performs suture ligation to secure the vessel.
The surgeon carefully dissects the gastroduodenal artery free, securing it with Hem-o-lok clips before dividing the artery.
The surgeon carefully dissects and mobilizes the inferior border and neck of the pancreas, revealing these anatomical structures within the surgical field.
An umbilical tape is placed posteriorly to the neck of the pancreas and used to provide traction, aiding in the manipulation and exposure of the pancreatic neck.

The surgeon transects the neck of the pancreas using monopolar scissors, while saline irrigation is applied to minimize tissue charring. Cutting current is used to reduce thermal coagulation of the pancreatic duct during the transection.

The surgeon creates a window in the transverse mesocolon to the left of the middle colic vessels, identifying the jejunum. The jejunum is transected fifteen centimeters distal from the ligament of Treitz using a robotic stapler.

The surgeon carefully dissects the uncinate process away from the superior mesenteric vessels. Branches from the superior mesenteric vein are meticulously dissected and suture ligated using six sutures.
The surgeon clips and divides the cystic duct and cystic artery, then transects the common hepatic duct just above the cystic duct takeoff.
The surgeon places the specimen into a retrieval bag and removes it through the umbilical trocar site, preparing it for pathology analysis.

The surgeon begins the reconstruction phase with a two-layer end-to-side pancreaticojejunostomy. The posterior layer is sutured using a 5-0 monofilament suture in a running fashion, connecting the pancreas to the jejunum.

A small enterotomy is made in the jejunum, and an anastomosis is created using interrupted 6-0 monofilament sutures. A small 7 or 5 French pancreatic duct stent is typically placed during this procedure.
The surgeon completes the anterior layer of the pancreaticojejunostomy using a running 5-0 monofilament suture. The entire anastomosis is then wrapped with a round ligament pedicle flap.
The surgeon creates a hepatico-jejunostomy approximately ten to fifteen centimeters downstream from the pancreatic jejunostomy, using five-oh monofilament sutures in a running or interrupted fashion.
The surgeon dissects the gallbladder free from the gallbladder fossa and detaches it from the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs an anticholic duodenal jejunostomy approximately fifty centimeters from the biliary anastomosis, using absorbable monofilament sutures in a running fashion.
Following the completion of the anastomosis, the surgeon removes the remaining specimens and places a suction drain in the right upper quadrant near the bile duct and pancreatic anastomosis.
